elements.sent-2411-001: To address the effective handling of high-dimensional data, the diversity of real noise, and the challenges in estimating standard deviation of the noise, we propose a novel and general graph-based square-root estimation (GSRE) model for sparse linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-002: Specifically, we use square-root-loss function to encourage the estimators to be independent of the unknown standard deviation of the error terms and design a sparse regularization term by using the graphical structure among predictors in a node-by-node form.
elements.sent-2411-003: Based on the predictor graphs with special structure, we highlight the generality by analyzing that the model in this paper is equivalent to several classic regression models.
elements.sent-2411-004: Theoretically, we also analyze the finite sample bounds, asymptotic normality and model selection consistency of GSRE method without relying on the standard deviation of error terms.
elements.sent-2411-005: In terms of computation, we employ the fast and efficient alternating direction method of multipliers.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, based on a large number of simulated and real data with various types of noise, we demonstrate the performance advantages of the proposed method in estimation, prediction and model selection.
elements.sent-2411-007: Sparse linear regression, square-root-loss function, graphical structure among predictors, alternating direction method of multipliers, oracle property.
elements.sent-2411-008: A very active research area in statistics is the linear regression problem, whose classical model is often assumed as $y=X \beta^* +\sigma\epsilon$, where $y\in \mathbb{R ^{n}$ is the observed response variable, $X\in \mathbb{R ^{n\times p}$ is the design matrix, $\beta^{*}\in \mathbb{R ^{p}$ is the true coefficient to be estimated, $\sigma\geq 0$ is the noise level, and $\epsilon\in \mathbb{R ^{n}$ is an error vector of i.i.d.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Many practical applications often encounter high-dimensional small-sample data, i.e., situations with small $n$ and large $p$, which will lead to the phenomenon of rank deficiency in the design matrix $X$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, the effectiveness of traditional least squares estimation model will be greatly challenged when the predictive variables are highly correlated or the data contains non-normal distributed noise.
elements.sent-2411-011: High-dimensional statistical modeling often employs sparse penalty methods, which involves adding different penalty functions to the square loss function $\|y-X \beta\|^2$ for variable selection, such as $\ell_1$ penalty \cite{T1996 , $L_p (0<p<1)$ quasi-norm \cite{FF1993 , smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) \cite{FL2001 , minimax concave penalty (MCP) \cite{Z2010 , etc. In addition, in some practical problems, such as sparse kernel learning \citep{KY2010 , additive modeling \cite{MVB2009 and multivariate regression \cite{F1991 , variable selection needs to be performed in the form of groups, which has led to the development of group penalty methods, such as group lasso \cite{BV2011,YL2006 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, its obvious limitation lies in the non-overlapping structure, which makes it unsuitable for the practical problems where features may be encoded in multiple groups, such as many biological studies on gene interactions \cite{LL2008,STM2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: To address the aforementioned issues, we have noticed that some literature have utilized the connectivity of an undirected graph to simulate the structural information among the predictors and improve sparse regression models based on this graph.
elements.sent-2411-014: Early literature mostly used edge-by-edge method, which employs penalty functions to promote similarity in regression coefficients for correlated predictors \cite{BR2008,KX2009,LL2008,YYLSWY2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, these methods did not directly utilize the neighborhood information of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, when there are more edges in the graph, this approach leads to more complex penalty terms.
elements.sent-2411-017: As an improvement, \cite{YL2016 proposed a node-wise penalty by using a node-by-node method.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, given the predictor graph $G$ and positive weights $\tau_i$'s, they defined the following norm \begin{equation}\label{gnorm} \big\|\beta\big\|_{G,\tau}=\min\limits_{\sum_{i=1}^{p}V^{(i)}=\beta, \supp(V^{(i)})\subseteq N_{i}}\sum_{i=1}^{p}\tau_i \|V^{(i)}\|_2,\end{equation} where $\supp(V^{(i) )$ is the support of vector $V^{(i)}\in \mathbb{R ^{p}$, $\|\cdot\|_2$ is the $\ell_2$ norm, and $\mathcal{N _{i}$ represents the set containing the predictor $i$ and its neighbors in graph $G$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: They no longer focus on the original variable $\beta$ of the model, but decompose $\beta$ into $p$ parts, and then use group sparsity idea based on predictor graph information.
elements.sent-2411-020: This design motivation comes from the explicit expression between the true coefficient and the inverse covariance matrix in analyzing least squares estimation.
elements.sent-2411-021: With the above notation, their optimization model in sparse regression incorporating graphical structure among predictors (SRIG) is given by \begin{equation}\label{YLmodel} \min_{\beta\in\mathbb{R}^p}\Big\{\frac{\|y-X \beta\|_{2}^2}{2 n}+\lambda \|\beta\|_{G,\tau}\Big\},\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is a regularization parameter.
elements.sent-2411-022: The SRIG method facilitates the inclusion of other predictors connected to an important predictor in a model, which is better suited to meet the demands of practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-023: Compared to penalty methods that do not use the graphical structure among predictors, SRIG method can significantly improve the performance of variable selection, estimation and prediction.
elements.sent-2411-024: The good performance of this research idea has attracted the attention of many scholars and has been extended to linear discriminant analysis \cite{LYL2019 , generalized linear regression \cite{ZZZ2019,LL2022,SAD2021 , expectile regression \cite{PZW2023 , quantile regression \cite{WLT2021 , etc. Despite these attractive features, there still exists a theoretical and practical challenge.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Because the aforementioned problems based on the smooth square loss usually need to assume that the noise follows a normal distribution, and the estimation performance depends on knowing standard deviation of the error terms in advance.

elements.sent-2411-027: However, in high-dimensional settings, accurately estimating $\sigma$ is a non-trivial task, and the types of noise generally exhibit diversity.
elements.sent-2411-028: Fortunately, the proposal of the square-root-loss function overcomes the aforementioned limitations, which was first proposed by \cite{O2007 .}

elements.sent-2411-030: It has been theoretically validated that SRL can achieve near-oracle property without knowing or estimating $\sigma$ in advance.

elements.sent-2411-035: In this paper, we carry out research on integrating the square-root-loss regression method and the penalty methods based on graphical structure among predictors.
elements.sent-2411-036: This research aims to construct a novel model for the classic high-dimensional linear regression problem, which can analyze the intrinsic information in data and alleviate the reliance on normal noise.
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, we propose the following graph-based square-root estimation (GSRE) model: \begin{equation}\label{pmodel1} \min_{\beta\in\mathbb{R}^p}\Big\{\frac{\|y-X \beta\|_{2}}{\sqrt{n}}+\frac{\lambda}{n} \|\beta\|_{G,\tau}\Big\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-038: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that square-root-loss regression incorporating graphical structure among predictors.
elements.sent-2411-039: Firstly, for several classes of predictor graphs with special structures, we verify the equivalence of the proposed GSRE method with several existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-040: Then, when the graphical structure $G$ of the predictors is known, we provide finite sample bounds, asymptotic normality and model selection consistency for the GSRE method with a $\sigma$-free tuning sequence $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-041: For the case where the graphical structure $G$ is unknown, we construct it by sparse estimating the covariance (or precision) matrix of the predictors \cite{CLL2011,FHT2008,YL2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: In terms of computation, we adopt the classical alternating direction method of multiplies (ADMM) in the optimization field.
elements.sent-2411-043: Simulation studies and practical applications demonstrate that the proposed GSRE method exhibits good performance in variable selection, model estimation and prediction.

elements.sent-2411-248: In this paper, we addresses the high-dimensional sparse linear regression problem by proposing a graph-based square-root estimation (GSRE) method, which integrates square-root loss and node-wise graph penalty.
elements.sent-2411-249: This paper highlights the broad applicability of the proposed method by analyzing its equivalence with several classical square-root estimation models.
elements.sent-2411-250: A notable feature of the theoretical analysis is that the tuning parameter no longer depends on the unknown standard deviation of the error terms.
elements.sent-2411-251: Additionally, we establish theoretical results on finite sample bounds and model selection consistency.
elements.sent-2411-252: In terms of computation, we use the fast and efficient alternating direction method of multipliers.
elements.sent-2411-253: Extensive simulation and real tests demonstrate that the proposed GSRE method is a competitive tool for estimation, prediction and model selection.
elements.sent-2411-254: Future work will consider extending this approach to more general loss functions, such as $\ell_p$ ($p\geq1$) loss \citep{W2013,XKLQ2018,ZW2015,WCN2018 , which will facilitate the handling of a broader range of noise types in high-dimensional settings.}

elements.sent-2411-365: Sparse linear regression is one of the classic problems in the field of statistics, which has deep connections and high intersections with optimization, computation, and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-366: To address the effective handling of high-dimensional data, the diversity of real noise, and the challenges in estimating standard deviation of noise, we propose a novel and general graph-based square-root estimation (GSRE) model for sparse linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-367: Specifically, we use square-root-loss function to encourage the estimators to be independent of the unknown standard deviation of error terms and design a sparse regularization term by using the graphical structure among predictors in a node-by-node form.
elements.sent-2411-368: Theoretically, we also analyze the finite sample bounds, asymptotic normality and model selection consistency of GSRE method without relying on standard deviation of error terms.
elements.sent-2411-369: Supplementary materials for this article are available online.
elements.sent-2411-370: High-dimensional statistical modeling often employs sparse penalty methods, which involves adding different penalty functions to the square loss function $\|y-X \beta\|^2$ for variable selection, such as $\ell_1$ penalty \citep{T1996 , $L_p (0<p<1)$ quasi-norm \citep{FF1993 , smoothly clipped absolute deviation (SCAD) \citep{FL2001 , minimax concave penalty (MCP) \citep{Z2010 , etc. In addition, in some practical problems, such as sparse kernel learning \citep{KY2010 , additive modeling \citep{MVB2009 and multivariate regression \citep{F1991 , variable selection needs to be performed in the form of groups, which has led to the development of group penalty methods, such as group lasso \citep{BV2011,YL2006 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-371: However, traditional group penalty methods often require non-overlapping structure between subgroups, which makes them unsuitable for the practical problems where features may be encoded in multiple groups, such as many biological studies on gene interactions \citep{LL2008,STM2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-372: To effectively handle group sparsity problems with overlapping structures between subgroups, we have noticed that some literature has utilized the connectivity of an undirected graph to simulate the structural information among the predictors and improve sparse regression models based on this graph.
elements.sent-2411-373: Early literature mostly used edge-by-edge method, which employs penalty functions to promote similarity in regression coefficients for correlated predictors \citep{BR2008,KX2009,LL2008,YYLSWY2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-374: As an improvement, \citet{YL2016 proposed a node-wise penalty by using a node-by-node method.}
elements.sent-2411-375: Specifically, given the predictor graph $G$ and positive weights $\tau_i$'s, they defined the following norm \begin{equation}\label{gnorm} \big\|\beta\big\|_{G,\tau}=\min\limits_{\sum_{i=1}^{p}V^{(i)}=\beta, \supp(V^{(i)})\subseteq N_{i}}\sum_{i=1}^{p}\tau_i \|V^{(i)}\|_2,\end{equation} where $V^{(i)}\in \mathbb{R ^{p}$ is a partition of $\beta$, and there may be overlapping parts among the $V^{(i)}$'s, $\supp(V^{(i) )$ is the support of vector $V^{(i)}\in \mathbb{R ^{p}$, $\|\cdot\|_2$ is the $\ell_2$ norm, and $\mathcal{N _{i}$ represents the set containing the predictor $i$ and its neighbors in graph $G$.}}}}
